article.title: AI-Discovered 'Copy Fail' Linux Kernel Flaw Grants Root on Almost Every Major Distribution Since 2017, CISA Adds It to KEV Within Days
article.summary: A 732-byte Python script can hand any local user root on Ubuntu, Red Hat, SUSE, and Amazon Linux. CISA gave federal agencies until May 15 to patch.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A logic flaw in the Linux kernel's cryptographic subsystem lets an unprivileged local user gain root on nearly every major distribution shipped since 2017, and the proof-of-concept exploit fits in a 732-byte Python script. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-31431 and nicknamed Copy Fail, was publicly disclosed on April 29 by researchers at the security firm Theori, who said they found the bug using an AI-powered scanning tool called Xint Code, [according to BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/). The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency added Copy Fail to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 1, giving federal civilian agencies until May 15 to patch, [as reported by The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html).

## What We Know

Copy Fail carries a CVSS score of 7.8 and is rated high-severity. The bug sits in the kernel's `authencesn` cryptographic template, [according to The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/), and [BleepingComputer reports](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/) that an attacker reaches it by combining the AF_ALG socket interface with the `splice()` system call to perform a four-byte controlled write in the page cache of a file. [SecurityWeek reports](https://www.securityweek.com/copy-fail-logic-flaw-in-linux-kernel-enables-system-takeover/) that the template, used to support IPsec Extended Sequence Numbers, ends up writing four bytes past the AEAD tag into cached copies of other files when the kernel's destination scatterlist contains page-cache pages.

The practical effect, as Theori's writeup puts it, is that "an unprivileged local user can write four controlled bytes into the page cache of any readable file on a Linux system, and use that to gain root," [per The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/). Because the kernel reads the page cache when loading binaries, an attacker can splice corrupting bytes into the in-memory copy of a setuid-root binary like `/usr/bin/su` and trigger privilege escalation the next time it runs, without ever touching the file on disk, [The Register notes](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/).

The public proof-of-concept is a 732-byte Python script of about 10 lines, [according to The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/), and Theori reports it works deterministically — no race conditions, no kernel-offset guessing — across Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, Amazon Linux 2023, Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.1, and SUSE 16, [BleepingComputer reports](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/).

The root cause is an in-place optimization introduced in the kernel's crypto code in 2017 that reused buffers instead of keeping input and output separate, [BleepingComputer reports](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/). [The Hacker News traces](https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/new-linux-copy-fail-vulnerability.html) the issue to a source-code commit in August 2017 in the `algif_aead` module. The upstream fix removes that optimization and reverts to out-of-place operation, [according to SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/copy-fail-logic-flaw-in-linux-kernel-enables-system-takeover/).

## Disclosure Timeline

Theori reported the bug to the Linux kernel security team on March 23, and patches became available within a week, with public disclosure following on April 29, [BleepingComputer reports](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/). The fix is shipping in stable kernel releases 6.18.22, 6.19.12, and 7.0, [as confirmed by The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html). Debian, Ubuntu, SUSE and Red Hat have all issued patches, [according to The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/).

Two days after disclosure, CISA added CVE-2026-31431 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 1, describing the bug as a "Linux Kernel contains an incorrect resource transfer between spheres vulnerability that could allow for privilege escalation," and ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply patches by May 15, [The Hacker News reports](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html). The Microsoft Defender Security Research team said it was "seeing preliminary testing activity that might result most likely in increased threat actor exploitation over the next few days," [per The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html).

## Why It Matters Beyond a Single Server

Copy Fail's design has unusually clean implications for shared-kernel infrastructure. SecurityWeek notes that the flaw poses particular danger in multi-tenant environments, shared-kernel containers, and CI runners executing untrusted code, [as reported by SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/copy-fail-logic-flaw-in-linux-kernel-enables-system-takeover/). Theori has separately said the bug "represents a potential container escape primitive that could affect Kubernetes nodes, because the page cache is shared across the host," [per The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/).

Unlike previous high-profile Linux LPE bugs such as Dirty Cow and Dirty Pipe, Copy Fail does not depend on winning a race condition, [The Register notes](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/). [The Hacker News describes](https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/new-linux-copy-fail-vulnerability.html) the exploit chain as opening an AF_ALG socket bound to `authencesn(hmac(sha256),cbc(aes))`, constructing shellcode, triggering a write into the cached copy of `/usr/bin/su`, and then executing the modified binary as root.

## What We Don't Know

CISA did not disclose details about active exploitation methods in the wild when adding the bug to KEV, [per The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html). It is also not yet clear how widely the vulnerability has been weaponized beyond the public proof-of-concept; Microsoft's statement points only to "preliminary testing activity" so far, [according to The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html).

The broader question is what the Copy Fail discovery says about AI-assisted vulnerability research. The Register reports that Theori researcher Taeyang Lee identified the vulnerability with the help of the company's AI security scanning software, Xint Code, [per The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/), but the security community has yet to assess how reproducible such results are when the same approach is pointed at other kernel components.

## Mitigations

[SecurityWeek reports](https://www.securityweek.com/copy-fail-logic-flaw-in-linux-kernel-enables-system-takeover/) that the risk concentrates wherever untrusted local code can run — multi-tenant environments, shared-kernel containers, and CI runners executing untrusted code. Patched stable kernels are tracked at 6.18.22, 6.19.12, and 7.0, [per The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html).
